content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Eric Krull on Unsplash

5 min read
Just now

--

Everyone’s talking about AI agents right now. Companies say they’re building “agentic workflows.” Tech influencers post about frameworks with names like LangGraph and CrewAI like they’re the only way in. It can feel like there’s a secret club, and you need a PhD to get past the door.
Here’s the truth: an AI agent is a much simpler idea than the hype makes it sound. In this post, I’ll show you what an agent actually is, and we’ll build a tiny team of three of them in plain, beginner-friendly Python, without touching a single framework. If you’ve never written code before, you’ll still be able to follow along. If you have, you’ll see just how little “magic” is really involved.
So, What Is an “AI Agent,” Really?
Strip away the buzzwords, and an AI agent is just three things put together:

• A job. A specific role, like “you are a researcher” or “you are an editor.”

• A brain. Usually a large language model (like the one powering ChatGPT or Claude) that can think through the job and produce an answer.

• A way to hand off work. The ability to pass what it produced to the next agent, or back to a human.
That’s it. There’s no hidden intelligence beyond what the underlying AI model already has. An “agent” is really just an AI model with a job description and a to-do list. When people talk about “multi-agent systems,” they mean several of these specialized AI models working in sequence, kind of like a small team passing a project down an assembly line.
Why Build a Team of Agents Instead of Just Asking One AI to Do Everything?
You could just ask one AI model to “research this topic and write me an article.” It would try. But it often does a mediocre job at everything, because it’s trying to be a researcher, writer, and editor all at once, in one breath.
Instead, imagine splitting that task the way a real newsroom would:

• One person researches the facts.

• Another person writes the draft based on that research.

• A third person reviews the draft and sends notes back if it’s not good enough.
Each person is better at their job because it’s the only job they’re doing. That’s the entire idea behind multi-agent systems. We’re not making the AI smarter. We’re just organizing its work better.
Meet Our Team: Researcher, Writer, and Critic
For this example, we’ll build three agents that work together to write a short explainer on any topic you give them:

• The Researcher gathers key facts and points about the topic.

• The Writer turns those facts into a short, readable draft.

• The Critic reads the draft and decides: is this good enough to publish, or does it need another round?
If the Critic isn’t happy, the draft goes back to the Writer with notes, and they try again, just like a real editing process.
The Building Blocks
Before we look at any code, here’s what’s actually happening under the hood, described without jargon:

• We write a short instruction for each agent, basically a note that says “you are the Researcher, your job is to find 4–5 key facts about the topic.”

• Each agent’s note gets sent to an AI model along with the current topic (or the previous agent’s work).

• The AI model responds with its part of the job: facts, a draft, or feedback.

• A simple loop in our code passes each response to the next agent in line, like handing a folder to the next desk in an office.
There’s no complicated infrastructure here. It’s a conversation between a few specialized instructions and an AI model, organized by a small amount of everyday code.
The Code (Don’t Worry, It’s Shorter Than You Think)
If you don’t write code, feel free to skim this part. The plain-English summary underneath each block tells you everything you need to know. If you do write code, this is literally all it takes.
from anthropic import Anthropicclient = Anthropic()
def ask_agent(role_instructions, task_input):
"""Send a job to one agent and get back its response."""
response = client.messages.create(
model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
max_tokens=500,
messages=[
{"role": "user", "content": f"{role_instructions}\n\n{task_input}"}
]
)
return response.content[0].text
This is just a small helper that says, “take this job description, take this input, and ask the AI model to respond.” We’ll reuse it for all three agents.
Now let’s define our three roles:
researcher_role = "You are a Researcher. List 4-5 key facts about the topic below."
writer_role = "You are a Writer. Turn the facts below into a short, friendly paragraph."
critic_role = "You are a Critic. If the draft below is clear and accurate, say 'APPROVED'. If not, explain what to fix."These are just three short notes describing each agent’s job. Nothing fancy, just clear instructions, the same way you’d brief a new employee.
And here’s the loop that ties it all together:
topic = "why the sky is blue"facts = ask_agent(researcher_role, topic)
draft = ask_agent(writer_role, facts)
for attempt in range(3):
feedback = ask_agent(critic_role, draft)
if "APPROVED" in feedback:
break
draft = ask_agent(writer_role, f"{draft}\n\nFeedback: {feedback}")
print(draft)
We give the Researcher a topic, pass its facts to the Writer, and let the Writer produce a draft. Then the Critic checks the draft. If it’s not approved, the Writer tries again using the Critic’s notes, up to three times. Once approved, we print the final result.
That’s the whole system. No framework, no special agent library, just three instructions, one helper function, and a loop.
What This Actually Looks Like When You Run It
Here’s a simplified version of what the team might produce for the topic “why the sky is blue”:

• Researcher returns facts about sunlight, the atmosphere, and how blue light scatters more than other colors.

• Writer turns that into something like: “The sky looks blue because sunlight is made of every color, and as it passes through our atmosphere, blue light scatters in all directions more than the other colors, so wherever you look, you see blue.”

• Critic reads it, decides it’s clear and accurate, and replies “APPROVED.”

• The final draft gets printed.
If the Critic had instead said “this doesn’t explain why other colors don’t scatter as much,” the Writer would try again with that note in mind, just like a real revision.
When You’d Actually Want a Framework
To be fair to tools like LangGraph or CrewAI, they’re not pointless. Once you’re managing dozens of agents, need them to use outside tools (like searching the web or reading files), or need to save and resume long-running conversations, a framework saves you from reinventing a lot of wheels.
But if you’re learning how agents work, or building something small and personal, starting with plain code like this teaches you what’s actually happening. You can always add a framework later, once you understand what it’s doing for you.
Try It Yourself
You don’t need to be technical to understand the idea. Break a big task into smaller jobs, give each job a clear brief, and let them hand work off to each other. That’s the whole concept behind “AI agents.” The rest is implementation detail.
If you want to experiment, the full code from this post is easy to copy and run with any AI provider’s API. Swap “why the sky is blue” for any topic you like, and watch your tiny AI team get to work.
